coredata.dc:title: CQDS preluded carbon-incorporated 3D burger-like hybrid ZnO enhanced visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity and mechanism implication
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jinze
Liu, Kai
Xue, Jinlong
Xue, Guanqin
Sheng, Xuejing
Wang, Huiqin
Huo, Pengwei
Yan, Yongsheng
coredata.dc:description:
Environmentally friendly photodegradation of refractory pollutants utilizing semiconductor photocatalysis evolution exhibits satisfactory efficiency and low energy consumption, but some of its unbefitting band position largely limits practical applications. The controllable band structure tuning via coupling low-dimensional materials with enormous potential in semiconductor photocatalysis, notably attractive carbon quantum dots (CQDs) induced for a better utilization of low-energy photoexcitation of promising inherent optical properties and photocatalytic performance on organic pollutant degradation. Here, a CQDs modified ZnO hybrid materials was synthesized and the CQDs preluded carbon-incorporated burger-like ZnO nanoparticles clusters were presented thus demonstrated a specific doping-effect. The dispersity improved by loading halloysite nanotube (HNTs) for efficiently photocatalytic activity enhancement. Furthermore, the band structure, excitation and photocatalytic reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation were detected to reveal the photochemical properties and plausible mechanism of CQDs hybrid nanomaterials system. It is observed that the superoxide radical (O2
−), and singlet oxygen(1O2) are the principal ROS agents under UV–vis excitations. This work not only displays a CQDs modified system of expanding photo-response range to visible light but also throws a way for quantum elemental incorporated on the influence of band-tuning for semiconductor- dominated photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Carbon-incorporated
ZnO
Photocatalysis
Tetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of zinc oxide (ZnO) and ZnO@ Halloysite nanotubes (HNTs)
Prefabrication of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), CQDs/ZnO and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21576125 , 21776117 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BK20151349 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2017M611716 and 2017M611734 ), Six Talent Peaks Project in Jiangsu Province (Grant No. XCL-014 ), Zhenjiang Science & Technology Program (Grant No. SH2016012 ), Postgraduate Scientific Research Innovation Project in Jiangsu Province (Grant No. KYCX18_2270 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology of the as-prepared ZnO-CQD-HNTs blocks. TEM of the ZnO particles (a), CQDs (b), burger-like CQDs/ZnO (c, d), ZnO@HNTs (e) and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs (f); HRTEM images of CQDs/ZnO heterostructure (g); EDX analysis (h) of CQDs/ZnO; SEM images (i) and EDX elemental mapping (j–m) of burger-like CQDs/ZnO.
XRD patterns of as-prepared HNTs, ZnO, ZnO@HNTs and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs.
XPS spectra of (a) survey, (b) Zn 2p, (c) C 1s and (d) O 1s of CQDs/ZnO@HNTs.
FT-IR spectra of as-prepared HNTs, ZnO, ZnO@HNTs and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of as-prepared HNTs, ZnO, ZnO@HNTs and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs. Inset: The band gap of corresponding samples. (b) XPS valence band spectra of pure ZnO and CQDs/ZnO.

(a) PL emission spectra of ZnO, ZnO@HNTs and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs. Inset: Expanded PL excitation spectra of corresponding samples. (b) The nanosecond-level time-resolved fluorescence decay dynamics of ZnO, ZnO@HNTs and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs monitored under a 418 nm laser excitation.
Transient photocurrent response (a) and EIS Nyquist plots (b) of pristine ZnO, ZnO@HNTs and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs nanocomposites under visible light irradiation.

(a) Evolution of TC relative concentrations (C/C0) with irradiation time for the blank, photolysis, ZnO, ZnO@HNTs, burger-like 16 mL-CQDs/ZnO and 16 mL-CQDs/ZnO@HNTs; (b) Evolution of TC relative concentrations (C/C0) with irradiation time for the CQDs/@ZnO@HNTs with different dosages of CQDs solutions. (c) Kinetic fits of ln(C0/C) as a function of irradiation time for the CQDs/ZnO@HNTs with different dosages of CQDs solutions. (d) Variation in the UV − vis absorption spectrum of TC aqueous solution before and after irradiating with light.

(a) Trapping experiments of active species during the photocatalytic degradation of TC by CQDs/ZnO@HNTs hybrid materials under visible light irradiation. ESR spectra from ZnO and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs hybrid nanostructures modified by CQDs for generation of ROS under simulated sunlight in the presence of DMPO detected for (b) hydroxyl radical (
OH), (c) superoxide radical (O2
−), and (d) the presence of 4-oxo-TEMP for detection of singlet oxygen (1O2). The control represents the sample containing spin probe and catalysts before exposing by simulated sunlight. All the ESR spectra were recorded after 10 min of irradiation with simulated sunlight (450 W Xeon lamp).
Schematic mechanism illustration of the band configuration and the charge separation during the photodegradation process in CQDs/ZnO@HNTs system under visible light irradiation.
Schematic illustration of the formation of CQDs/ZnO@HNTs in the whole synthetic process.

head.ce:title: CQDS preluded carbon-incorporated 3D burger-like hybrid ZnO enhanced visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity and mechanism implication

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineer, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineer

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation of CQDs/ZnO@HNTs in the whole synthetic process.

Environmentally friendly photodegradation of refractory pollutants utilizing semiconductor photocatalysis evolution exhibits satisfactory efficiency and low energy consumption, but some of its unbefitting band position largely limits practical applications. The controllable band structure tuning via coupling low-dimensional materials with enormous potential in semiconductor photocatalysis, notably attractive carbon quantum dots (CQDs) induced for a better utilization of low-energy photoexcitation of promising inherent optical properties and photocatalytic performance on organic pollutant degradation. Here, a CQDs modified ZnO hybrid materials was synthesized and the CQDs preluded carbon-incorporated burger-like ZnO nanoparticles clusters were presented thus demonstrated a specific doping-effect. The dispersity improved by loading halloysite nanotube (HNTs) for efficiently photocatalytic activity enhancement. Furthermore, the band structure, excitation and photocatalytic reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation were detected to reveal the photochemical properties and plausible mechanism of CQDs hybrid nanomaterials system. It is observed that the superoxide radical (O2
−), and singlet oxygen(1O2) are the principal ROS agents under UV–vis excitations. This work not only displays a CQDs modified system of expanding photo-response range to visible light but also throws a way for quantum elemental incorporated on the influence of band-tuning for semiconductor- dominated photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: The inability of conventional catalysts to dispose a wide spectrum of pollutants is a critical unsolved problem for water treatment. Increasing attention has been focused on semiconductor nanomaterials for photodecomposition of toxic water contaminants and coping with the problem of energy crisis [1,2]. The central issue of the recent photocatalysis research was demonstrated a commitment to the expansion of the optical absorption to visible wavelength range for raising the utilization of solar energy. Heretofore, numerous semiconductor materials have been gained gratifying achievements act as photocatalysts, such as TiO2, BiOI, ZnIn2S4, WO3 and In2O3 [3–6].

During the long course of development of metallic oxide semiconductors photocatalysts, zinc oxide (ZnO) have been widely studied as a kind of gorgeous materials for sewage purification due to the functionally productiveness, less energy consumption and environmental sustainability [7]. However, too wide band gap (3.2 eV) of which takes advantage of barely 5% energy of solar pure photons to gain the photo-response for sunlight, thereby restricting its widespread use [8,9]. What’s more, ZnO particles possess the smaller aggregation rate as to render enough chance to twist so as to further decreases surface energy from the chemical kinetic view point in higher viscosity non-aqueous medium [10]. For this reason, there exist many studies such as constructing various heterostructure by coupling with feasible band structure semiconductors, doping with metallic or non-metallic ions, or sensitizing treatments have been carried out to enhance the thermodynamic driving force for oriented attachment to form nanospheres and promote visible photo-response activity of ZnO-based photocatalysts [11–13].

Currently, the metal-free carbonaceous materials like carbon nitride, carbon nanotube, graphene or graphdiyne uniting with ZnO have been constantly studied for efficacious photocatalysis [14–17]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which grown rapidly in nanocarbon family nearly five years, have taken centre stage owe to the distinctive optical performance and electronic properties [18,19], as well as excellent biocompatibility, great aqueous solubility, low chemotoxicity, adjustable photoluminescence for which have been successfully applied in biomedical, Biochemical sensing, optoelectronics, and photoelectron-catalysis [20]. Despite an acknowledged expositions for the mechanism of fluorescence of CQDs have not been determined completely, considerable lens have been focused on enhancement of fluorescence quantum yield (QY) for CQDs, and numerous evolutions have been studied [21,22]. The CQDs with expansive band light absorption were endued by their quantum effect. Thus, using as light absorbers to improve photocatalytic performance, CQDs have been applied to couple with semiconductor nanoparticles for photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants [23–25]. Besides, CQDs also have a role of the charge acceptor or transshipment to conduct the photo-generated carriers ascribe to the π-conjugated structure of CQDs [26]. Recently, some CQDs dressed semiconductor systematic photocatalysis have been discussed, and several improved activities were got, such as BiOCl, CdS, TiO2, g-C3N4, Cu2O, and ZnFe2O4 [27–32]. However, there still retained necessary to further improve the performance of CQDs and its composite. Due to the small size of CQDs, the modified CQDs could have enough interface combining with ZnO nanoparticles and the intimate connection of such the nanoscale heterojunctions would be constructed [7]. The charge migration duration and channels have been prolonged by the ubiquitous CQDs, thus promoting the process of interfacial charge migration [23,33]. Simultaneously, the excessive light blocking effect can be availably reduced by the highly dispersed CQDs, as to ensure the efficient light utilization [34]. Considering the fact that the CQDs possess numerous attractive properties, the strategy of constructing and introducing CQDs modified ZnO by a suitable approach has been conceived to enhance capabilities of photocatalysts.

As one type of naturally magnesium silicate clay minerals, Halloysite nanotubes (HNTs), with a unique compound of nanotubular structures, large specific area, mild utilizability, abundant functionalities, kind biocompatibility, and high mechanical strength, have received increasing research attention in various fields [35,36]. For the sake of preventing from aggregation of semiconductor materials, HNTs also serve as a facilely obtainable and low-cost supporter with favorable chemical constitution of interface for the accessory of semiconductor nanomaterials [8].

Herein, we reported a novel CQDs/ZnO@HNTs heterostructure composite by precipitation-hydrothermal process for served to photodegrading tetracycline (TC) under visible light. Interestingly, the morphology of initial ZnO particles have been assembled to 3D uniform burger-like mesoporous ZnO after coupling with CQDs. The same pattern has been reported present to a carbon-incorporated ZnO nano-architectures prepared via a common solvothermal method in the presence of glucose in ethylene glycol (EG) solution [10]. In our study, we experimentally demonstrate, the carbon-incorporated preluded by introducing CQDs which with an environmentally benign microwave method as described in our previous study [23]. Notably, the process of synthesis did not involve any toxicant or surfactants, and CQDs not only act as director of carbon incorporating also give a crucial role of integrating precursor in weakening the force of nano-building blocks to inhibit the direct fusing of ZnO space lattice among nanoparticles so that promoting them self-assembly orderly. Furthermore, a believable fabrication mechanism was proposed based on present analysis for the assembly process. With the CQDs preluded carbon incorporation loading at HNTs, with the increased surface area and surface functional groups that giving rise to enhancing interfacial electrostatic interaction would further enhance the adsorption capability [37]. Meanwhile, Reactive oxygen species (ROS), typically including superoxide radicals, hydroxyl radicals and singlet oxygen, have been proposed as the dominant reactive species answerable for the performance of semiconductor nanoparticles photocatalysis [38]. The as-obtained CQDs/ZnO@HNTs display a homologous effect of exotic in photocatalytic generated ROS depend on visible absorbed excitation script which result in especial generation of singlet oxygen (1O2). Such the photo-response of ZnO can also enlarge toward visible light region that leading to the enhanced degradation efficiency of antibiotics. The overall procedure for fabrication of 3D burger-like ZnO clusters and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs were shown in Scheme. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of zinc oxide (ZnO) and ZnO@ Halloysite nanotubes (HNTs)
ce:section.ce:para: ZnO and ZnO@HNTs photocatalyst precursor was prepared by chemical precipitation method. In a typical procedure, 4 mmol ZnCl2 was dispersed in 60 mL absolute ethanol, and then 0.486 g HNTs was added into the above-mentioned mixed solution with vigorous stirring. After that, 100 mL of 0.08 M LiOH·H2O ethanol solution was dropwise added to the above prepared solution under ultrasonic processing. Lastly, the precursor was collected and rinse with deionized water and ethanol for several times via centrifugation, and then vacuum dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prefabrication of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), CQDs/ZnO and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, the CQDs aqueous solution was synthesized by a modified microwave method according to our previous research [23]. 2.2 g of citric acid monohydrate was dissolved into 40 mL deionized water involving 1049 μL of ethanediamine with magnetic stirring, followed by using microwave method with a constant power about 850 W and heating at 180 °C for 15 min. Lastly, ZnO and ZnO@HNTs photocatalyst precursor were added to a certain amount of CQDs solution, respectively, that were placed in 100 mL Teflon-sealed autoclaves and held at 160 °C for 6 h. When being cooled at room temperature in air, the procured solids were secluded by filtration, washed with distilled water and absolute ethyl alcohol a few times, and finally drying under vacuum at 70 °C for 12 h.

The microstructures and composition of catalysts were investigated by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and scanning TEM (STEM)-EDX elemental mapping operated on a JEOL, JEM3000F (300 kV). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were carried out via a Rigaku D/max-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Cu-Kα source, scanning angle range of 5–80°, 5°·min−1). A Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer was occupied to analyze UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) in the range of 200–800 nm with BaSO4 powder as the reference. Surface electronic states were detected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi, Al Kα X-ray source), the binding energies were corrected by the C1s peak at 284.6 eV from surface adventitious carbon. Electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements were performed by Brucker A300 ESR spectrometer to investigate the hydroxyl radicals (
OH), superoxide radicals (
O2 −) and singlet oxygen (1O2) detection at room temperature.

In this study, the tetracycline (TC), as one of symbolic antibiotics, was used to examine the performance of the as-fabricated photocatalysts samples. A 500 W Xenon lamp with λ > 420 nm was equipped during the photocatalytic tests in whole. Briefly, 80 mg of as-prepared photocatalysts were dispersed into 100 mL TC solution (20 mg·L−1) in a cylindrical reactor with condensate circulation water under the constant temperature (25 °C). All of the suspensions with continuous stirring in the dark condition for 30 min for reaching an adsorption/desorption balance before 90 min visible-light illumination. At a certain interval, 4 mL aliquots were collected and with immediately centrifugation. Finally, the filtrates of degraded TC solution were determined at a maximum absorption wavelength (357 nm) via the UV–vis spectrophotometer. The degradation activities could be calculated as follows:
degradation
efficiency
%
=
1 -
C t
/
C 0
× 100 %
=
1 -
A t
/
A 0
× 100 %
where C0 represent the initial concentration of the TC solution in adsorption/desorption balance and Ct signify remaining concentration after irradiation, respectively. And which At and A0 corresponding to the TC absorbance values.

To determine the crystalline structure and morphology of the as-prepared samples, the TEM, SEM, HR-TEM and EDX elemental mapping images were depicted. Fig. 1 a and 1b displays the TEM images of as-obtained ZnO and CQDs, respectively. It could be seen that the as-obtained ZnO particles were aggregated spherical nanoparticles and CQDs with regular size of 2–5 nm. As shown in Fig. 1c and 1d, the carbon-incorporated CQDs/ZnO were clearly exhibited with burger-like. Fig. 1e and 1f show the TEM images of as-prepared ZnO@HNTs and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs with an excellent dispersion, respectively, and as-prepared sample retain a favorable minor-size burger-like morphology after CQDs modified. As shown in the HRTEM image (Fig. 1g), the crystalline structure and heterojunction of as-prepared ZnO/CQDs could be observed obviously, from which matches well with wurtzite ZnO with the lattice spacing at 0.26 nm. And for the as-obtained CQDs of composite involved both graphitic regions and amorphous carbon with the lattice spacing about 2.30 Å which corresponding to the (1 0 0) intralayer of the typical CQDs (inset of Fig. 1g) [10,39]. The EDX analysis and SEM images of CQDs/ZnO were shown in Fig. 1h–m. The distribution of C, Zn and the C elements in the prepared hybrid material in the EDX mapping images (Fig. 1k, l, m) were further confirmed that the CQDs introduced carbon-incorporated mostly exist independently or on the surface of the nanosized as-prepared materials.

Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of ZnO and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs with different CQDs loading amounts The distinct peaks located at ca. 31.75°, 34.46°, 36.18°, 47.54°, 56.62°, 62.70°, 68.08° and 69.07° are indexed to the (1 0 0), (0 0 2), (1 0 1), (1 0 2), (1 1 0), (2 0 0), (1 1 2) and (2 0 1) crystal planes of hexagonal ZnO phase, respectively [40]. It was indicated that all the diffraction peaks of pristine ZnO were matched with the standard XRD patterns of the hexagonal wurtzite ZnO structure with lattice constants of a = 3.25 Å and c = 5.207 Å (JCPDS file No. 05-0664), whereas the ZnO@HNTs had characteristic diffraction peaks of HNTs (Halloysite 8 Å) identified with JCPDS file No. 29-1487 [8,41]. No diffraction peak shift was observed while the CQDs were modified on ZnO@HNTs. However, no typical diffraction peak of CQDs was detected due to the limited amounts of CQDs in the CQDs/ZnO@HNTs composites and alike results could also be found in previous reports [42,43]. In addition, the average crystallite size of ZnO nanoparticles in the CQDs/ZnO@HNTs composites was estimated about 10 nm according to the Scherrer equation [44], that were corresponding with TEM images.

In order to detect the presence of CQDs and possibly expose the surface electronic states of synthesized samples, XPS was carried out for the CQDs/ZnO@HNTs and CQDs/ZnO. The survey spectrum (Fig. 3 a) confirms that the CQDs/ZnO@HNTs samples contain C, O and Zn elements, and the peaks of C 1s, O 1s and Zn 2p appeared at standard positions. As shown in Fig. 3b, the peaks centered at about 1046.2 eV and 1023.4 eV are attributed to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2, respectively [14,45]. The C 1s core level spectrum is presented in Fig. 3c and the broad peak is located at the binding energy of 285.2 eV. The C 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum showed one major peak with binding energies at 284.6 eV, which could be ascribed to the CC bond (graphitic carbons) with sp2 orbital [46]. The other two peaks with binding energies at 286.6 eV and 287.8 eV are assigned to oxygenated carbons (the CO bond with alcoholic carbon, the CO bond with carbonyl carbon or the OCO bond with carboxyl carbon) and nitrous carbons (the CN bond with acylamino carbon), respectively, which revealed the surface functional groups on the CQDs [47,48]. For the O 1s core level spectrum in Fig. 3d, the O 1s spectrum can be fitted into three peaks located at 529.2 eV, 531.4 eV and 533.2 eV, which are attributed to O2− ions in the ZnO bonding of the wurtzite ZnO structure, oxygen-containing groups (OH, CO, CO) and the oxides of HNTs simplified into an integrated peak, respectively, drawing the same conclusion of the successful loading of CQDs in CQDs/ZnO@HNTs composites [49–51]. The XPS analysis further indicated the CQDs have been modified on the ZnO@HNTs materials and carbon-incorporated intimate integration has been achieved.

For examining the chemical composition of the obtained products, FT-IR spectra of the HNTs, ZnO, ZnO@HNTs and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs are shown in Fig. 4 . With respect to HNTs sample, the absorption bands at 3696.9 cm−1 and 3623.6 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibrations of OH in the inner-surface hydroxyl groups of AlOH [36]. The strongest absorption peaks at 1097.3 cm−1, 1029.8 cm−1 and 912.2 cm−1 belong to the SiO stretch vibration, the SiOSi asymmetrical stretch vibration and the AlOH bending vibration, respectively [52]. Two characteristic bands of interlayer or adsorbed water at 3450.0 cm−1 and 1641.2 cm−1 were presented, which due to the stretching vibration of OH and the corresponding bending vibration, respectively [48]. For ZnO, the broad absorption band at 468.6 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of ZnO and the weak peak located at 875.5 cm−1 is due to the bending vibration of ZnO [53,54]. The successful decoration of CQDs can be proven by comparing the FT-IR spectra of ZnO@HNTs and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs. The characteristic absorption peaks of CO, CC and CN stretching vibration are located at 1702.9 cm−1, 1602.6 cm−1 and 1396.2 cm−1, respectively [24,42]. This indicated that CQDs and ZnO@HNTs have been coupled together successfully.

UV–vis spectrometer was applied to investigate the optical absorption that is related to the efficiency of photodegradation. Fig. 5 a shows the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the as-prepared HNTs, ZnO, ZnO@HNTs and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs samples. The Kubelka-Munk equation was applied to measure the optical band gap (Eg), and then a plot was obtained via the transformation based on [F(R∞)hν]1/2-hν for direct band gap semiconductors [55]. The pure ZnO sample only has optical absorption at UV light region and exhibited the abrupt onset of absorption at about 387 nm, corresponding to the band gap of about 3.2 eV [7]. Distinct from the pure ZnO absorption behavior, the obtained ZnO@HNTs composites exhibited wide visible light absorption region, which is the result of the introduction of HNTs. Nevertheless, the band gap energy of ZnO@HNTs composites remains the same with pure ZnO. After the CQDs were introduced, the CQDs/ZnO@HNTs sample showed the highest absorption in visible light region and the absorption offset occurs at about 439 nm, giving the band gap of 2.82 eV, which indicated its enhanced visible light induced photocatalytic activity [56]. Noticeably, the visible light harvesting of CQDs/ZnO@HNTs composites has increased significantly in comparison with that of pure ZnO and ZnO@HNTs, obviously demonstrating that the enhanced light absorption of CQDs/ZnO@HNTs composites can make effective utilization of visible light and thus result in the production of more electron-hole pairs under the same light irradiation [31,57]. Effective masses at the top of valence band and at the bottom of conduction band were deduced from the band structure [58]. Specifically, the relative energy levels of valance band maximum (VBM) and conduction band minimum (CBM) would be significant for the redox capacity of photoinduced carriers in the photocatalytic system [59]. To further determine the energy band structure of as-prepared materials, the XPS valance band spectra was then performed. As shown in Fig. 5b, the highest occupied electronic density of states was measured to be 2.79 eV which matched for initial ZnO by XPS valance spectra. The VBM of CQDs/ZnO was measured to be 2.775 eV that slightly smaller than pure ZnO, which indicating that the introduction of CQDs did exactly change the electronic density of states of ZnO matrix. It is also implying that the CQDs not only existed in the form of surface modification but also doping into the ZnO which may initiate the electronegative defects states [56,60]. These results were consistent with previous HRTEM and EDX elemental mapping analysis.

To investigate the influence of introduced CQDs on the photocatalytic activity, photoluminescence spectroscopy studies were performed, through which the migration and recombination processes of photoinduced electron-hole pairs on each sample could be found out as PL emission arises from the recombination of free carriers [18,39,55]. Fig. 6 showed the steady-state PL spectra of the pure ZnO, ZnO@HNTs and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs samples, and the PL spectra of burger-like CQDs/ZnO has been shown in SI. The pure ZnO materials exhibits a strong green emission located at 529 nm under the excited at 408 nm, which mainly caused by the high recombination rate of free excitons of ZnO [12]. In addition, the intensity of PL signal of pure ZnO is higher than ZnO@HNTs composites, illustrating that the recombination probability of ZnO@HNTs is smaller than that of pure ZnO. After the introduction of CQDs, the PL emission intensity of CQDs/ZnO@HNTs composites is obviously lower than that of ZnO@HNTs for the extended lifetime of charge carriers and the reduced recombination rate of electron-hole pairs, indicating that CQDs/ZnO@HNTs composites has higher photocatalytic activity. This phenomenon is attributed to the high electron transmission of spatially extended π-bonding conjugated system of CQDs [27,30]. The fluorescence lifetime of ZnO, ZnO@HNTs and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs was analyzed by time-resolved diffuse fluorescence decay spectra are shown in Fig. 6b exponentially. CQDs/ZnO@HNTs exhibits slower decay kinetics compared with other two photocatalysts of three. Two radiative lifetimes with different relative percentages have been calculated by fitting biexponential equation insert of Fig. 6b. It can be observed that the short lifetime of as-obtained samples are 1.62 ns, 1.21 ns and 0.89 ns, respectively, which the emission lifetime of CQDs/ZnO@HNTs (0.89 ns) is shorter than that of ZnO (1.62 ns) and ZnO/HNTs (1.21 ns). Meanwhile, obvious PL quenching and lifetime decrease indicate the formation of an electron transfer pathway from ZnO to CQDs in a nonradiative quenching manner [61]. The relatively higher decay lifetime of CQDs introduced indicates rapider interfacial charge transfer in the photocatalytic system. Moreover, for the addition of CQDs, the efficient interfacial charge migration which have been significantly accelerated of hybrid materials, due to an ultrafast electron transfer (ET) process and decrease the recombination of e−/h+ pairs in the interfacial layer of CQDs/ZnO@HNTs [62–64]. In conjugation with these results, it concluded that in the case of CQDs/ZnO@HNTs composite photocatalyst resulting in the highest photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation.

In order to explore the photoelectrochemical performances of as-obtained samples, the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carrier was probed by photoelectrochemistry analysis on an electrochemical station (CHI852C Newport 69920) with solar simulator (300 W Xenon lamp). The photoelectric response and EIS measurements were conducted in Fig. 7 . The transient photocurrent responses of pure ZnO, ZnO@HNTs and 16 mL-CQDs/ZnO@HNTs were determined in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution for several on–off cycles under visible-light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). The 16 mL-CQDs/ZnO@HNTs exhibits much stronger transient photocurrent response which approximately six times larger than that of ZnO@HNTs (Fig. 7a). The result implies that CQDs play a crucial role for efficiency charge transport and finally as a reason of the higher photocurrent response, the separation of e−/h+ pairs photogenerated in ZnO is significantly enhanced [65]. It is worthwhile to note that little loss of photocurrent (ΔI) exists during the irradiation duration of visible light, which can be rationally attribute to the consumption of electrons including the trapping of electrons by O2 and the recombination of electron-hole pairs [29,66]. The EIS Nyquist analysis (Freq = 0.1 Hz ∼ 100 kHz, 0.5 V) have examined as shown in Fig. 7b. The arc radii of 16 mL-CQDs/ZnO@HNTs electrode are smaller than those of pristine ZnO and ZnO@HNTs electrodes with light on and off, indicating that a faster surface reaction rate of the working electrode, the whole photocatalysis system possesses more effective charge transfer and favor lower rate of charge recombination after the addition of CQDs [19,39].

The photocatalytic activity of these as-obtained samples was tested by photodegrading a kind of antibiotic, tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) as the model pollutant with vis-light. The current concentration of TC at varied intervals were examined via surveying the absorbance with UV–vis spectra at 357 nm. Before the photocatalytic tests, the adsorption–desorption equilibrium of all the samples were achieved by magnetically stirring in the dark for 30 min absorption procedure. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the activity of as-prepared burger-like CQDs/ZnO was not optimal, with the introduction of different amounts of mineral HNTs as the carrier of ZnO, the photocatalytic activity obviously improved 28% due to that of well-dispersion. It has been determined that the optimal photocatalytic activity of CQDs/ZnO@HNTs was successfully synthetized quantified as 16 mL CQDs with the degradation of 92.48% in 90 min via visible light irradiation from Fig. 8b. These demonstrated that the CQDs preluded an efficacious effect of carbon-incorporated for improving the photocatalytic performance of ZnO. While the CQDs dosages were overmuch about 20 mL, there caused a decreased photocatalytic performance, which can be attributed excess CQDs adhere to the surface of ZnO would restrict the light absorption of photocatalytic semiconductor and reduce the contact region in the simulated contaminant -photocatalysts system [24]. Fig. 8c shows a linear relationship between ln (C0/Ct) and degradation time, displaying that the photoactivity of TC keep to the first-order kinetics. The pseudo-first-order rate constants (k) for different amounts of CQDs samples are estimate from the slope, which same as previous degradation tests. Moreover, the frequent recombination of electron-hole pairs give rise to a bright surface defect-derived fluorescence of CQDs which strongly localized π-π* electronic levels of the sp2 sites, all of sites result in strong visible emissions and serve as a recombination site for photo-generated electron and holes [30,67]. The same conclusion could also be obtained from FL analysis (Fig. 6). Therefore, the photocatalytic property of CQDs/ZnO@HNTs with an enhancement be ascribed to the existence of CQDs that as more charge carriers and excitors are available. Based on the above discussion, it can be accepted that the 16 mL CQDs/ZnO@HNTs have shown the highest activity for the photodegradation of TC under visible-light irradiation.

To confirm the probable mechanism, the active species trapping experiments were employed via the photodegradation of TC by adding trapping scavengers for optimal CQDs/ZnO@HNTs under visible-light irradiation. Several kinds of scavengers, p-benzoquinone (BQ), isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and triethanolamine (TEOA), were selected for superoxide radical (O2
−), hydroxyl radical (
OH) and holes detection, respectively [68]. As presented in Fig. 9 a, the photodegradation of TC was evidently depressed with addition of the TEOA and BQ, which verified the O2
− and holes played the essential roles during the photocatalytic process. Nonetheless, the photodegradation activity reduces slightly in the presence of IPA. The above results indicate that the photogenerated O2
− and holes are the dominant reactive active species of CQDs/ZnO@HNTs.

Moreover, ESR spin trapping and spin labeling are two most direct effective recognized inspection techniques for the detection of short-lived free radicals and paramagnetic species were carried out to identify each ROS and electron/holes generated during excitation conditions of CQDs/ZnO hybrid nanocomposite under UV–vis light illumination. Here, we select 5,5-diamethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trap for the
OH and O2
−, 4-oxo-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (4-oxo-TEMP) for detection of singlet oxygen (1O2) [69,70]. Fig. 9b–d illustrates the ESR spectra contained various trapping agents from ZnO and CQDs/ZnO@HNTs hybrid materials before and during irradiation of simulated sunlight, and no obvious ESR signal could be observed for the control samples. First, there is no clear ESR signal such a typical 1:2:2:1 quartet characteristic spectrum assigned to the adducts formed between DMPO and the hydroxyl radical (DMPO/·OH), which confirmed that no
OH has been generated in the suspension. Conversely, a characteristic senary consecutive and 1:1:1 triplet signals were observed obviously under irradiation in Fig. 9c and d, in accordance with that of DMPO/O2
− and TEMPONE
, which verified the O2
− and 1O2 generation by ZnO or CQDs introduced composite [38]. To verify the generated singlet oxygen ESR signal, presence of sodium azide (NaN3) as an efficient scavenger for 1O2 was detected [71]. The triplet ESR signals was decreased greatly with addition of NaN3 for ZnO or CQDs/ZnO@HNTs irradiated thus confirming the previous results of production of 1O2 (data not shown). Furthermore, the ESR signal intensity of 1O2 generated by 16 mL-CQDs/ZnO@HNTs photoexcited were calculated about 1.5 times from pristine ZnO, indicating that incorporated CQDs with ZnO effectively enhanced generation of 1O2. The result of above ESR and radical trapping experiments analysis concluded that the main active species were detected be O2
− and holes in the CQD/ZnO@HNTs system.

As the results discussed above, it is obviously that compared to ZnO, the CQDs/ZnO@HNTs hybrid nanomaterials demonstrated a greatly enhanced photoactivity in generated superoxide, singlet oxygen, photoinduced holes, which consistent with the introduced CQDs preluded carbon-incorporated ZnO clusters and dispersity of HNTs. Notably, the enhancement effect of CQDs in this system is not only to act as an electron reservoir for hindering the recombination, the controllable band structure promotion which induced by the formation of CQDs-incorporated CQDs/ZnO@HNTs system is crucial for turned the excitation wavelengths to nicely match the expected visible light region. The mechanism for the enhancement would be determined by analyzing the coupled modes of CQDs between the interface of ZnO as result in generation of reactive oxygen species. The band edges of correlative ZnO materials were calculated by the empirical equation EVB = X − Ee + 0.5Eg and ECB = EVB − Eg
[72]. As shown in Fig. 10 , the pure ZnO with a wide band gap (Eg = 3.19 eV), has the conduction band edge and valence band edge that calculated to be −0.305 and 2.885 eV (vs normal hydrogen electrode (NHE)), respectively. According to the DRS analysis (Fig. 5a), after the introduce of CQDs, the band gap of modified ZnO has been narrowed about 0.37 eV from 3.19 eV to 2.82 eV. Therefore, the conduction band bottom and valence band top of can be estimated as −0.12 and 2.70 eV. The details of band edge positions of series materials are shown in Fig. 10. It is remarkably that after the CQDs-ZnO junction formed, the CB of modified ZnO is lower and the VB value is less positive than that of pristine ZnO. The similar tendency can also be observed by XPS valence band spectra in Fig. 5b. In addition, the VB and CB value of semiconductors materials determine the type and strength of ROS generated by photoexcitation [66,73]. The VB value of modified ZnO (2.70 eV) was more positive than E0 (
OH/OH−) (2.38 eV vs NHE) and the CB value of that (−0.12 eV) was negative than E0 (O2/O2
−) (−0.046 eV vs NHE). It is worth mentioning here that the alteration of band edge position before and after modification of ZnO have certainly effected the generation of suggested reactive species, as revealed by the corresponding ESR spectra [74].

On the basis of the above results, the plausible mechanism for greatly enhanced visible-light-driven photocatalytic performance in this system could be attributed to several aspects: (1) By utilizing various and specific band structure of coupling with CQDs, like the up-conversion effect and acting in such a way as the photogenerated hole-acceptor, which has been investigated for CQD-based hybrid materials [30,34,67]. When photoexcited under visible light irradiation, the electrons in the HOMO level of CQDs could jump to the LUMO level of CQDs due to the enough energy obtained, and then transferred to the CB of ZnO. The holes left in the HOMO of CQDs where they oxidize H2O and organic should be oxidized [75–77]; (2) As the previous characterization of as-prepared materials, herein, it is known that the CQDs initiated the carbon incorporated to ZnO which take advantages of CQDs for their elemental abundance. With the carbon incorporated in the system, a negative shift of Fermi energy level of ZnO which caused by CQDs incorporated, result in the photogenerated electron in CB of ZnO can easier be transferred for reacting with dissolved oxygen to produce superoxide anions [78,79]. Remarkably facilitates the conversion of 3O2 to 1O2 via resonance energy transfer that the efficient generation of excited excitons in CQDs/ZnO@HNTs [73].

In conclusion, we have experimentally demonstrated the carbon incorporated ZnO via coupling CQDs during the synthetize process of that. A notable transformation of burger-like CQDs/ZnO morphology was observed and the HNTs have been introduced to improve the dispersity of hybrid photocatalysts. A representative detection during the photoexcitation process of CQDs/ZnO@HNTs have been equitably analyzed via photo-luminescence spectroscopy. From efficiently modulation of band structure of CQDs-based materials, the as-prepared CQDs/ZnO@HNTs display a remarkable optical excitation in photogenerated reactive oxygen species (ROS), which demonstrated the superoxide radical (O2
−), holes and singlet oxygen (1O2) are the dominant active species products under UV–vis light excitations. This work provide an efficient and novel strategy for element incorporated with introduced inorganic quantum dots owing to the quantum size effect. Meanwhile, it also developed a new sight of CQDs participated phase tuning for semiconductors, thus offering a beneficial route for expanding the application of VLD photodegradation based on band-tuning semiconductors and other low-dimension materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21576125, 21776117), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BK20151349), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2017M611716 and 2017M611734), Six Talent Peaks Project in Jiangsu Province (Grant No. XCL-014), Zhenjiang Science & Technology Program (Grant No. SH2016012), Postgraduate Scientific Research Innovation Project in Jiangsu Province (Grant No. KYCX18_2270).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcat.2018.11.026.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
